title: Add SLO→Customer Impact Classifier & Remediation Workflow doc (v2 scoring + updated comms)
preview:
Context
We have an internal Confluence page describing the SLO→customer impact tier classifier and the remediation workflow. That page is being referenced during incidents, but the operational details have drifted since the enrichment pipeline shipped score weighting changes (v2) and CS escalation timing was tightened after SUP-19344.

This PR adds the canonical doc to the slo-toolkit repo so it can be versioned alongside the classifier code and reviewed via normal PR workflows. The majority of the content mirrors the Confluence page, but includes the v2 scoring thresholds and updated templates.

What changed (summary)
- Keep: tier definitions (1–4), overall workflow (alert→enrich→score→tier→remediate), evidence retention requirements, and post-incident/error budget reconciliation flow.
- Update: score mapping thresholds (v2), add confidence gating and a human-review fallback, update Tier 1 comms SLA (10 min), and reduce Tier 4 auto-escalation window (4 hours).
- Update: SLO catalog excerpt targets to match current baselines; add explicit note that targets are examples and owners must use the live SLO registry as source of truth.
- Update: capacity knobs table to reflect current CLI commands and pool naming.

Doc content (added under docs/incident-response/)

Title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow

Overview
This document defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 250ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defined rolling window.
- Customer-impact tier: a 1–4 classification used to route and prioritize incident response.

Customer-impact tiers (quick guide)
| Tier | User-visible symptoms | Business effect | Escalation urgency | Owner to notify |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Multi-region outage OR widespread request failures OR sustained elevated error rate affecting a large portion of traffic | Severe revenue/customer retention impact | Immediate (page) | SRE lead + Product PM + CS Director |
| 2 | Single-region outage, or large subset of customers impacted (major tenants) or major feature down | Significant business disruption for key customers | High (page + slack) | SRE oncall + Product owner + CS manager |
| 3 | Localized errors, degraded latency for non-critical endpoints | Minor customer annoyance, limited support tickets | Medium (slack alert) | On-call SRE + Feature owner |
| 4 | Minor latency increase or transient errors with no customer tickets | No immediate business impact | Low (ticket) | On-call SRE |

SLO catalog excerpt (example mapping; owners must confirm in live SLO registry)
| SLO name | Metric | Target | Error-budget window | Default customer-impact tier |
|----------|--------|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| request_latency_p95_public | request_latency_p95{route=~"/v1/.*"} | <= 250ms | 30d | 2 |
| api_error_rate_5m | sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) | <= 0.5% | 7d | 1 |
| embedding_throughput_tail | embedding_infer_latency_p99 | < 1.4s | 14d | 3 |
| console_ui_availability | synthetic_check(console_login) | 99.7% monthly | 30d | 3 |

Classification algorithm (operational)
1) Alert receives telemetry: detector alerts on SLI breach or error-budget burn (alerting config).
2) Metrics enrichment: pull impact signals (affected tenant count, region distribution, top N tenants by RPS, open support tickets matching pattern).
3) Heuristics engine evaluates scores (0–100): failure-scope, severity, customer-exposure.
4) Confidence gating (v2): compute confidence (0–100) based on completeness/consistency of enrichment inputs. If confidence < 50, do not auto-upgrade beyond Tier 3; route to on-call for manual triage and re-run enrichment after 5 minutes.
5) Combined score maps to Tier (v2 thresholds): Score >= 85 -> Tier 1; 70–84 -> Tier 2; 35–69 -> Tier 3; < 35 -> Tier 4.
6) Attach classification + confidence to incident and follow the tiered remediation workflow below.

Heuristics inputs (examples)
- affected_tenant_ratio: percent of total tenant RPS impacted.
- p95_latency_delta: percent change vs baseline.
- error_rate_delta: absolute change in error rate vs baseline (preferred over p99_error_spike for API).
- active_cs_tickets: number of open tickets tagged /inference or /api.
- region_blast_radius: number of regions with concurrent impact.

Operational remediations by tier
Tier 1 (Immediate response)
- Page the SRE oncall and SRE lead (PagerDuty service: customer-impact).
- Open an incident in the incident-management system using template tag=CUSTOMER_IMPACT_TIER_1 (auto-created if classifier confidence >= 70).
- Run mitigation playbook: fallback-to-compatible-model-variant.
- If capacity-related: trigger immediate capacity burst policy (see CLI below).
- Notify CS and Product leadership within 10 minutes with templated Slack/Jira.

Tier 2 (High-priority)
- Page on-call (high urgency) and post to #incidents.
- Execute targeted throttles or tenant fair-share configuration.
- Begin canary rollback of recent model or config changes (if correlated).

Tier 3 (Operational handling)
- Create ticket and schedule on-call investigation within 30–60 minutes.
- Apply mitigations from the runbook: restart worker pool, clear KV cache, or disable newly-deployed feature flag.

Tier 4 (Monitoring)
- Monitor metrics and let automated remediation run. If unresolved after SLA window (4 hours), escalate to Tier 3.

Sample alert rule (Prometheus-style YAML)
alert: HighAPIErrorRate
expr: sum(rate(api_errors_total[5m])) / sum(rate(api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.005
for: 2m
labels: severity: page impact: "possible-customer-impact"
annotations: summary: "API error rate >0.5% over 5m" runbook: "https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation"

Enrichment query examples (pseudo-SQL / analytics)
- Tenants affected in last 10m: SELECT tenant_id, count(*) FROM requests WHERE status >= 500 AND timestamp > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT 50;
- Support ticket correlation: count tickets where created_at >= now()-10m AND tags IN ('api','inference')

Capacity escalation knobs (examples)
| Trigger | Condition | Automated action | Escalation threshold |
|---------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| headroom_exhausted | available_gpu_slots < 7% | trigger-burst-alloc --pool=shared-burst-v2 | immediate (Tier1) |
| model_warm_pool_empty | warm_pool_size < configured_min | scale warm pool by +2 nodes | Tier2 |
| p95_latency_spike | p95 > baseline*1.6 for 10m | enable-prefetch-kv-cache=true | Tier2 |

CLI examples
- Trigger burst capacity (operators only): rcmd infra capacity burst apply --pool shared-burst-v2 --reason "Tier1 SLO breach" --expires 30m
- Re-route traffic to fallback models: rcmd infra routing promote --from model-xyz-v2 --to model-xyz-v1 --weight 100

Communication templates
Slack template (Tier1)
- Channel: #incidents
- Message: [Tier 1 SLO breach] api_error_rate_5m at 1.2% impacting ~32% of tenant RPS across 2 regions. IC: @oncall. Mitigation in progress: promoting fallback routing. Please notify CS lead @renee.zhao. Incident: <INCIDENT_URL>

Jira template fields
- Priority: P0 for Tier1, P1 for Tier2, P2 for Tier3/4
- Components: runtime, routing, capacity, product-feature
- Labels: slo-breach, customer-impact-tier-<n>, automated-enrichment, classifier-v2

Post-incident / Error-budget reconciliation
1) Add classification evidence (metrics, tenant list, tickets) to the incident story.
2) Compute error-budget consumption attributable to the incident window.
3) For Tier1/Tier2 incidents: schedule a joint SRE/Product/CS triage within 72 hours. The triage determines whether to take a governance action (e.g., customer credits, temporary throttle, emergency capacity commit).
4) Document remediation plan and assign owners with due dates.

Postmortem checklist (to include in incident review)
- Summary of classifier result (tier + confidence) and whether the initial tier matched customer reports.
- Timeline of detection -> mitigation -> recovery.
- Root cause and contributing factors including capacity and SLO misconfigurations.
- Action items (owner + due date) and follow-up on error-budget policy.
- Evidence attachments: metrics, logs, enrichment query results.

Automation and operational tooling notes
- The classifier runs in the telemetry enrichment pipeline (owner: telemetry-team). Inputs are Prometheus alerts, request traces, and support ticket API.
- All automated classification decisions must be auditable: store computed scores, confidence, and input summaries in incident metadata.
- Confidence gating exists to prevent false Tier 1/2 upgrades when enrichment is degraded (e.g., ticket API latency, tenant-mapping lag).

Ownership and governance
- SLO owner: Each SLO must list a primary owner (SRE or Product) and a secondary owner. Owners are responsible for maintaining mapping in the SLO registry and ensuring this doc stays consistent.
- Classification engine owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com.
- Incident review owner for Tier1/Tier2: SRE lead on rotation.

Runbook links (examples)
- API error mitigation: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/api-error-rate-mitigation
- Fallback routing: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/fallback-routing-and-rollbacks
- Capacity burst playbook: https://confluence.internal/s/reliability/runbooks/capacity-burst-and-burst-rollback

Change log
- 2026-01-15 — Initial draft published (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-20 — Added capacity knobs and CLI examples (review: Liam O'Connor).
- 2026-03-01 — Finalized communication templates and post-incident checklist (review: Renee Zhao).
- 2026-03-16 — Updated classifier thresholds to v2, added confidence gating, tightened Tier1 notification SLA to 10 min, and reduced Tier4 escalation window to 4 hours (review: Sasha Kim).

Appendix: sample incident evidence payload (illustrative)
- incident_id=INC-2026-0316-014 slo=api_error_rate_5m failure_scope=81 severity=88 customer_exposure=63 confidence=77 mapped_tier=1 top_affected_tenants=[tenant-acme,tenant-vela] evidence_queries=[tenant_impact_query_2026-03-16-10m]

Operator note
If you are an on-call SRE and this workflow appears to misclassify an incident, create a ticket in the SRE backlog and tag telemetry-team for review. Include the incident link and the classifier scores/confidence from incident metadata.

Contact
- Document owner: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.com)
- Telemetry owner: telemetry-team@redwood.com
- Incident management: incidents@redwood.com

Testing
- N/A (documentation only).

Rollout
- Once merged, we will link this doc from the internal runbook index and update the Confluence page to point at this repo copy as the source of truth.

title: Compact call-tree capture and on-call primer seeder
preview:
Motivation: On-call responders frequently need a quick top-down view of what the service process looked like at incident start (goroutine/thread hotspots, recent exec path, open network sockets) and a focused, action-oriented primer tailored to the observed signals. This PR introduces a lightweight call-tree collector and an on-call primer seeder that bundles captured artifacts with heuristic remediation hints so first responders can act faster without full trace access.

What this does: 1) Introduces a compact call-tree capture agent (golang) that samples active goroutines+stack traces, condenses them into a weighted call-tree (hot path aggregation), and attaches minor environment fingerprints (listening ports, recent open files, binary version). 2) Adds a seeder component (python) that consumes the compact capture and generates a short on-call primer: probable root causes, 3 next diagnostic commands, safe mitigations, and suggested runbook links. 3) Adds Slack and CLI integration so the collector+seeder output can be pushed to the incident channel or downloaded as a .zip. 4) Feature-flagged and opt-in by default for Private/Dedicated clusters (configurable via SLO_TOOLKIT_CAPTURE=true).

Design notes: - Compact format: top 50 call frames aggregated into a tree with counts and a simple entropy score for nondeterministic noise. Designed to be <= 100KB for standard services. - Privacy: the collector strips string literals and environment variables unless an explicit allowlist is set; binaries/paths are hashed when sent to central seeder. - Extensibility: seeder uses a small rule engine (yaml-driven) to map call-tree fingerprints to runbook templates; initial rule-set covers HTTP worker exhaustion, DB connection leaks, event-loop stalls, and native OOM patterns.

Checklist: - [x] Collector implementation (golang) with sampling + aggregation - [x] Seeder implementation (python) + rule engine - [x] Slack + CLI delivery hooks - [x] Unit and integration tests - [x] Documentation page and runbook templates - [x] Feature flag and opt-in defaults - [x] Sanitization and size guarding

Backward/compatibility: Opt-in; does not change default telemetry or network flows unless enabled. No breaking change to existing APIs.

Security and privacy: Strings are redacted by default. The seeder runs in the control plane (or optionally local) and never expands compressed attachments without explicit operator acknowledgment.

How to review: Focus on the collector's sampling window and aggregation correctness, and on the rule engine mappings for false-positive risk. The seeder's sample outputs are included in tests/fixtures for quick review.

Related work: ENG-4821 (incident triage speedups), SRE-119 (runbook seeding), OPS-337 (private deployment opt-in requirements).

title: heuristic DAG runbook compiler and local repro hints
preview: Motivation: Oncall responders frequently need a concise, executable set of steps that adapt to observed telemetry. This PR introduces a heuristic DAG compiler that converts high-level runbook templates and telemetry predicates into compact conditional playbooks plus short local repro hints (minimal traces, query snippets, and service call stubs). Design: 1) A lightweight DAG IR captures nodes (check, query, mitigation, escalate) and edges with guard predicates; 2) Compiler heuristics prune nodes unlikely to apply given recent telemetry (configurable thresholds) and collapse linear chains into single composite actions to reduce cognitive load; 3) Local repro hint extractor synthesizes a minimal set of queries/trace spans and a docker-compose sandbox recipe (when available) to reproduce the symptom locally; 4) Safe-mode runner executes a dry-run passing through the DAG with simulated telemetry to validate branch coverage. Checklist: - added DAG IR and in-memory evaluator; - implemented three pruning heuristics (frequency, recency, impact score); - added repro-hint extractor with sanitizers and redaction rules; - added unit tests for compiler, evaluator, and sanitizer; - added integration test that runs compiler on 20 existing runbooks and compares size reduction and coverage; - added small CLI `slo runbook-compile --input ... --dry-run` and a `slo runbook-local-repro` helper; - documented new config knobs in docs/runbooks/README.md; - feature gated behind feature flag `runbook_dag_compiler` and rollout plan in this PR. Testing & rollout: Unit tests pass locally and in CI. Integration runs showed a median runbook size reduction of 42% for the sampled corpus while preserving coverage for configured test prompts. Canary rollout: enabled for a 5% oncall group for two weeks, monitor false-positive rate and responder feedback. Migration: existing runbooks are still usable; CLI and API will accept both legacy and DAG format for 3 months before deprecating legacy authoring path. Safety and security: repro hints are sanitized using the project's redaction policy; any hint that would include PII or secrets is replaced with placeholders and an automated review flag is raised. Impact: intended to speed triage and lower MTTR by delivering more focused instructions and an ability to run quick local replays.
text: Motivation: Oncall responders frequently need a concise, executable set of steps that adapt to observed telemetry. This PR introduces a heuristic DAG compiler that converts high-level runbook templates and telemetry predicates into compact conditional playbooks plus short local repro hints (minimal traces, query snippets, and service call stubs). Design: 1) A lightweight DAG IR captures nodes (check, query, mitigation, escalate) and edges with guard predicates; 2) Compiler heuristics prune nodes unlikely to apply given recent telemetry (configurable thresholds) and collapse linear chains into single composite actions to reduce cognitive load; 3) Local repro hint extractor synthesizes a minimal set of queries/trace spans and a docker-compose sandbox recipe (when available) to reproduce the symptom locally; 4) Safe-mode runner executes a dry-run passing through the DAG with simulated telemetry to validate branch coverage. Checklist: - added DAG IR and in-memory evaluator; - implemented three pruning heuristics (frequency, recency, impact score); - added repro-hint extractor with sanitizers and redaction rules; - added unit tests for compiler, evaluator, and sanitizer; - added integration test that runs compiler on 20 existing runbooks and compares size reduction and coverage; - added small CLI `slo runbook-compile --input ... --dry-run` and a `slo runbook-local-repro` helper; - documented new config knobs in docs/runbooks/README.md; - feature gated behind feature flag `runbook_dag_compiler` and rollout plan in this PR. Testing & rollout: Unit tests pass locally and in CI. Integration runs showed a median runbook size reduction of 42% for the sampled corpus while preserving coverage for configured test prompts. Canary rollout: enabled for a 5% oncall group for two weeks, monitor false-positive rate and responder feedback. Migration: existing runbooks are still usable; CLI and API will accept both legacy and DAG format for 3 months before deprecating legacy authoring path. Safety and security: repro hints are sanitized using the project's redaction policy; any hint that would include PII or secrets is replaced with placeholders and an automated review flag is raised. Impact: intended to speed triage and lower MTTR by delivering more focused instructions and an ability to run quick local replays.

title: compact-shift-briefs: lightweight handoff digest and stratified probe sampler
preview:
Summary: This PR introduces `shift-brief`, a lightweight CLI and library for creating compact on-call handoff digests, plus a companion `stratified-probe-sampler` that schedules small, targeted telemetry probes during shift boundaries.

Motivation: long handoff notes and oversized telemetry bundles increase cognitive load for responders and slow down triage during rotations. The goal here is to reduce the reading time and bandwidth for on-call handoffs while retaining actionable context and safe, opt-in telemetry links.

Implementation highlights:
- New CLI command `shift-brief generate` (flags: --window, --severity-threshold, --sanitize-level) that emits a 3–5 sentence summary, prioritized list of active incidents (id, severity, 1-line status), and 1–2 sanitized example traces. Summaries are generated by heuristics tuned for recent incident activity and enriched with SLO drift metadata.
- Stratified probe sampler service: a background worker that picks candidate probes stratified by rotation role, recent failure modes, and historical mean-time-to-detect. Probes are intentionally lightweight (synthetic request pings, targeted trace captures limited to 5 spans, metric delta checks) and attached as ephemeral URLs (signed, 24-hour) into the brief.
- Privacy & safety: automatic redaction/sanitization pipeline runs on candidate attachments (removes PII, secrets, IP addresses) and an opt-out flag per-team. We publish the sanitization audit log as part of the brief for transparency.
- Integrations: hooks for incident-bot to surface brief links on handoff, and slo-toolkit dashboards to display brief health metrics (probe success rate, average digest size).
- UX defaults: by default the brief targets a 300–800 byte plaintext summary + up to 3 attachments. Users can configure verbosity per rotation via team config.

Checklist:
- [x] CLI implementation and unit tests
- [x] Probe sampler worker and sampling policy
- [x] Sanitization pipeline with audit log
- [x] Integration tests with incident-bot minimal flow
- [x] Docs and example team config
- [x] Backwards-compatible defaults and opt-out path

Commits (high-level):
- feat(cli): add `shift-brief` command and generator
- feat(sampler): add stratified probe sampler and scheduling policy
- feat(safety): add sanitization and attachment signing
- test: add unit and integration tests
- docs: add usage guide and config examples

Files changed (high level):
- cli/shift_brief.py: main CLI + summary generator
- sampler/worker.py: probe selection and scheduler
- sanitizer/redactor.py: sanitization passes and audit log
- integrations/incident_bot_hook.py: minimal link-posting helper
- docs/oncall/shift-brief.md: usage and config
- tests/*: unit + integration test cases
- config/defaults/shift_brief.yaml: default team config

Review thread (selected):
1) Ethan Brooks (reviewer): "Nice encapsulation — two questions: (a) how do you prevent noisy probes from increasing pager noise, (b) can the brief include a deterministic pointer to the canonical playbook?" Author (Sofia): "a) probes are stratified and micro-limited; success/failure of probes is sampled and aggregated, we also enforce a rate cap per-team. b) yes — updated the integration hook to include canonical_playbook_url if available; default is omitted to avoid stale links."

2) Priya Natarajan: "Sanitizer looks good but add explicit unit tests for IP and header redaction (edge cases)." Author: "Added targeted tests for CIDR ranges, IPv6, and Authorization-like header patterns (see tests/sanitizer_test.py)."

3) Liam O'Connor: "Minor: CLI help text could be clearer about the signed link expiry." Author: "Help text updated; default expiry now documented and configurable via env var SHIFT_BRIEF_LINK_TTL."

4) CI flake: one integration test failed on first run (flaky external metrics stub). I re-ran CI and all checks passed. Final approval from Ethan and Priya, then squash-merged.

CI summary:
- lint: pass
- unit: pass
- integration: pass (one rerun)

Safety note: Teams using on-prem/private deployments must enable the opt-in flag for attachment signing if they do not accept external signed URLs; config and docs include example to store attachments in a private S3 endpoint.

Benchmarks:
- Median handoff reading time reduced by 48% in internal bench (sample of 120 handoffs)
- Average brief payload size down 70% versus full snapshot export
- Probe success rate 97% in canary testing across three teams (lightweight probes only)

Migration notes:
- New team config file (defaults/shift_brief.yaml) will be applied to new orgs; existing orgs are opt-out and see no change until they enable `shift_brief.enabled: true`.
- Incident-bot hook requires a small ACL permission to post ephemeral links; operator instructions in docs/oncall/shift-brief.md

Rollout plan:
1) feature flag off by default for all orgs
2) enable for internal teams and two pilot customers
3) gather telemetry for 2 weeks, then enable on broader cohort if noise < 2% increase
4) GA and add to console toggles for admins

title: Introduce alert-load-simulator and alert-bias-metrics for on-call calibration
preview:
Background and motivation:
We frequently see skew between alert load observed during traffic spikes and the expected on-call burden derived from static alert rates. That mismatch causes under-provisioned paging windows and creates noisy escalation paths. This PR adds a lightweight alert-load-simulator and a small suite of alert-bias-metrics to help SREs and product teams reason about how configuration changes, SLO adjustments, and alert tuning affect on-call load under realistic failure and traffic patterns.

What this PR does (high level):
- Adds a simulator harness (slo_toolkit.simulator) that can replay historical incidents or generate synthetic traffic/failure scenarios to estimate page counts, paging latency distributions, and concurrency of alerts.
- Ships a set of alert-bias metrics (slo_toolkit.bias) which estimate the difference between observed page volume and expected volume under baseline traffic; these are designed to be exported to Prometheus and visualized in Grafana.
- Includes a small CLI and example k8s job (charts/alert-sim-example.yaml) so teams can run short simulations against their alert rules without deploying new code.
- Adds comprehensive unit and integration tests plus a smoke benchmark that validates memory/CPU bounds on representative simulation runs.

Why now:
- ENG-2148 and SRE-42 flagged repeated incidents where alert tuning had unexpected downstream on-call noise during region-level outages. The simulator allows us to reproduce those conditions in CI and during postmortems.

Files and components changed (summary):
- slo_toolkit/simulator/runner.py: core harness and scenario scheduler.
- slo_toolkit/simulator/generators.py: synthetic traffic/failure generators.
- slo_toolkit/bias/metrics.py: bias estimator and Prometheus exporter shim.
- cli/alert_sim.py: CLI entrypoint and config parsing.
- charts/alert-sim-example.yaml: Kubernetes job example for running simulations as a batch job.
- tests/test_load_sim.py, tests/test_bias_metrics.py: new tests.
- docs/alert-sim.md: usage and interpretation guide.

Usage and acceptance criteria:
- Teams can run `python -m slo_toolkit.simulator.runner --profile sample` and receive a JSON summary containing expected pages/hour, p95 page latency, and estimated operator concurrent alerts.
- Prometheus exporter exposes `slo_alert_bias_ratio` and `slo_alert_bias_delta` with labels for service/team/alert_rule.

Backward compatibility and rollout:
- All changes are additive. Exported metric names are new and do not collide with existing metrics. The CLI is opt-in and the k8s example is under docs/.
- We will roll out by creating a sherpa-run job for two pilot teams (payments, notifications) and collect bias metrics for two weeks.

Testing performed:
- Unit tests for generators and bias calculator.
- Integration test that runs a 2-minute synthetic storm scenario in CI (marked smoke) and verifies memory < 2GB.
- Manual validation using replay of a historical incident dataset supplied by SRE-ops.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc linked (ENG-2148)
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs updated
- [x] CI passing

Potential follow-ups:
- Dashboard templates for Grafana (follow-up PR)
- Automated alert-tuning suggestions driven by simulator outputs

Notes for reviewers:
- The simulator is intentionally conservative in resource assumptions to keep it runnable in CI. The benchmarking smoke harness is under tests/benchmarks and is configured to skip on low-CPU CI runners.

Rollback plan:
- The changes are non-invasive; to roll back remove the k8s example and stop using the new metrics. No alert rules are changed by this PR.

title: Stratified alert degrader and structured handoff manifest
preview: Context: Pager fatigue remains a major operational cost for services with noisy, low-impact alerts. This PR implements a stratified alert degrader that automatically reduces noisy signals to digestible summaries or lower-churn channels when they don't change SLO posture and responders have an elevated load. It also introduces a structured 'handoff manifest' artifact that the on-call rotation system and runbook generator can consume to present a compact, actionable snapshot for the incoming shift. What this PR does: 1) New degrader engine (slo-toolkit/alert_degrader) with pluggable policies based on SLO impact, recent incident frequency, and on-call availability. 2) Exporter that synthesizes a JSON handoff manifest (slo-toolkit/handoff/manifest_v1) containing prioritized alerts, suggested runbook entry, last-triage snapshot, and responsible owners. 3) CLI entrypoint (slo-manifestctl) and a small daemon mode to push manifests to the handoff queue. 4) Playbook exporter updates to include manifest pointers and degradation annotations. Motivation: Reduce unnecessary wakeups while preserving rapid response for true SLO-impacting regressions. Design notes: Degrader is opt-in per-service via a single config flag; policies default to conservative thresholds. The degrader never supresses alerts that cross SLO error budget boundaries; instead it changes routing/urgency. Tests: Unit coverage for policy evaluation, integration test with mock oncall API, e2e that validates manifest schema and runbook linking. Commits: feat(degrader): initial engine and policy framework; feat(handoff): manifest schema + exporter; cli: manifestctl and daemon scaffold; tests: unit + integration + e2e; docs: runbook examples and operator guide. Files changed (high level): alert_degrader/index.ts, handoff/manifest_v1.proto, cli/manifestctl.ts, exporters/playbook_exporter.ts, tests/integration/degrader.spec.ts, docs/operator/handoff-guide.md, ci/workflows/manifest-e2e.yml. Review summary: First iteration implemented core flow and schema; Rosa asked for telemetry labels to be normalized and to add a small counter metric for degraded alerts per-service; addressed in second commit. Priya requested clearer failure-mode behavior for handoff pushes (fallback to S3 store) and an operator-facing toggle; added in daemon mode. Liam suggested shrinking manifest payload by moving large attachments to ephemeral store and including a pointer; implemented as manifest.attachments[]. Outcome: Approved after two iterations, CI green, merged by squash. Merge method: squash. Related work: ENG-4821 (alert noise reduction initiative), PM-193 (oncall handoff improvements).

title: Playbook canary runner and sandboxed rehearsal CLI
preview:
Motivation: Runbooks are frequently edited without a lightweight way to validate that the sequence of steps and automated actions behave as expected. This PR introduces a canary runner for scheduled playbook rehearsals and a sandboxed CLI (slo-rehearse) that lets oncall and SREs dry-run playbooks against a mocked environment. Goals: 1) validate runbook logic and step ordering, 2) surface flaky remediation actions before hitting production, 3) provide safe rehearsal that exercises telemetry and alerting hooks without creating real incidents.

What this PR does: adds a sandboxed rehearsal CLI (slo-rehearse) that boots an ephemeral sandbox with mock providers, a playbook-canary scheduler/runner that can be configured per-service to run a subset of playbooks as periodic canaries, and a set of safety gates and reversible mocks so rehearsals cannot modify production state. The CLI supports --dry-run, --seed=<scenario-id>, and --snapshot-output to persist a sanitized log bundle for postmortem or developer review.

Checklist: [x] unit tests for core runner logic [x] integration tests against sandbox cluster (CI) [x] docs + quickstart for oncall runbooks [x] RBAC and config gating for enablement per team [x] telemetry events and metric names consistent with observability naming docs

Related: Linear ENG-4321 (playbook canary MVP), ENG-4410 (rehearsal telemetry and dashboards). Support ticket SUP-2104 requested a safer way to validate emergency runbooks.

Notes on safety: rehearsals default to project-scoped mocked providers. Any playbook step that calls an external mutating API must register a reversible mock adapter (pkg/safety/mocks.py) or the runner will mark the step as SKIPPED. The canary scheduler will only run on dedicated sandbox namespaces unless explicitly flagged by org admins.

Usage examples: `slo-rehearse run --playbook=svc-http-incident --seed=timeout-http` or from Console: schedule -> playbooks -> create canary runner.

This description is intentionally compact but covers the user-facing features, config knobs, and operational constraints.

title: add-synthetic-alert-replayer-and-slo-drift-detector-with-cli-integration
preview:
Motivation: On-call engineers frequently need to understand whether recent config/traffic changes produced real SLO regressions or flurries of noisy alerts. This PR introduces two complementary utilities in slo-toolkit: (1) a synthetic alert replayer that can replay historical alert batches against current routing/alerting logic (useful to validate alert rules and noise filters), and (2) an SLO drift detector that computes lightweight drift signals across sliding windows and surfaces candidate regressions with example traces and suggested runbook links. Both are exposed via a small CLI shim (sloctl replay / sloctl drift) and wired into the existing incident-bot runbook hinting path so that alerts replayed in a dry-run mode can annotate runbook suggestions with a confidence score.

What this PR does (single continuous description): Adds a new package slo_toolkit/replay that ingests alert-event ndjson exports (schema-compatible with our incident-bot export format), normalizes timestamps, and replays them through the current alert-routing/filtering logic. The replayer supports three modes: dry-run (no side effects; returns diagnostic report), validation (runs evaluator checks and emits pass/fail per rule), and inject (optionally re-emits matching alerts to a synthetic topic for downstream subscribers). Adds an SLO drift detector (slo_toolkit/drift) that computes percentiles and error budget velocity across sliding windows (1h/6h/24h) and emits prioritized drift candidates when thresholds and hysteresis are exceeded. The detector includes a lightweight scoring heuristic combining signal magnitude, trend persistence, and recent config changes (derived from the change_log index). A new CLI shim (sloctl) exposes replay and drift subcommands with consistent flags (--window, --sample-rate, --since, --dry-run, --output-format). Integration: incident-bot now consumes the replay dry-run report to annotate runbook suggestions with two new metadata fields: confidence_hint and replay_examples (up to 5 example alerts with matched rule IDs). Observability: adds Prometheus metrics (slo_toolkit_replayer_run_total, slo_toolkit_drift_candidates_total, slo_toolkit_replay_example_count) and an instrumented tracing span for end-to-end replay runs. Tests: unit tests for normalization and scoring heuristics, an integration test that runs a small historical export through the replayer, and a property-style test that ensures the drift detector is stable across synthetic noise.

Checklist: [x] unit tests added and passing locally; [x] integration test harness updated; [x] CLI docs stubbed; [x] metrics added and documented in observability.md; [x] incident-bot integration added with feature flag slo_replay_integration=false by default.

Backward compatibility and safety notes: CLI defaults to dry-run; incident-bot integration is behind a feature flag and will not change production behavior until enabled. The inject mode requires explicit opt-in and additional RBAC checks documented in the SECURITY.md additions.

Operational runbook: Added a short runbook fragment (docs/runbooks/replayer-runbook.md) describing when to run replays, typical false-positive patterns, and how to interpret drift scores.

text:
Motivation: On-call engineers frequently need to understand whether recent config/traffic changes produced real SLO regressions or flurries of noisy alerts. This PR introduces two complementary utilities in slo-toolkit: (1) a synthetic alert replayer that can replay historical alert batches against current routing/alerting logic (useful to validate alert rules and noise filters), and (2) an SLO drift detector that computes lightweight drift signals across sliding windows and surfaces candidate regressions with example traces and suggested runbook links. Both are exposed via a small CLI shim (sloctl replay / sloctl drift) and wired into the existing incident-bot runbook hinting path so that alerts replayed in a dry-run mode can annotate runbook suggestions with a confidence score.

What this PR does (single continuous description): Adds a new package slo_toolkit/replay that ingests alert-event ndjson exports (schema-compatible with our incident-bot export format), normalizes timestamps, and replays them through the current alert-routing/filtering logic. The replayer supports three modes: dry-run (no side effects; returns diagnostic report), validation (runs evaluator checks and emits pass/fail per rule), and inject (optionally re-emits matching alerts to a synthetic topic for downstream subscribers). Adds an SLO drift detector (slo_toolkit/drift) that computes percentiles and error budget velocity across sliding windows (1h/6h/24h) and emits prioritized drift candidates when thresholds and hysteresis are exceeded. The detector includes a lightweight scoring heuristic combining signal magnitude, trend persistence, and recent config changes (derived from the change_log index). A new CLI shim (sloctl) exposes replay and drift subcommands with consistent flags (--window, --sample-rate, --since, --dry-run, --output-format). Integration: incident-bot now consumes the replay dry-run report to annotate runbook suggestions with two new metadata fields: confidence_hint and replay_examples (up to 5 example alerts with matched rule IDs). Observability: adds Prometheus metrics (slo_toolkit_replayer_run_total, slo_toolkit_drift_candidates_total, slo_toolkit_replay_example_count) and an instrumented tracing span for end-to-end replay runs. Tests: unit tests for normalization and scoring heuristics, an integration test that runs a small historical export through the replayer, and a property-style test that ensures the drift detector is stable across synthetic noise.

Checklist: [x] unit tests added and passing locally; [x] integration test harness updated; [x] CLI docs stubbed; [x] metrics added and documented in observability.md; [x] incident-bot integration added with feature flag slo_replay_integration=false by default.

Backward compatibility and safety notes: CLI defaults to dry-run; incident-bot integration is behind a feature flag and will not change production behavior until enabled. The inject mode requires explicit opt-in and additional RBAC checks documented in the SECURITY.md additions.

Operational runbook: Added a short runbook fragment (docs/runbooks/replayer-runbook.md) describing when to run replays, typical false-positive patterns, and how to interpret drift scores.

Related: builds on ideas from ENG-4520 (alert noise playbacks) and SRE-310 (SLO regression monitoring).

title: Add audit log delivery SLO (latency + error rate) with enterprise-tier burn alerts
preview:
Summary
This PR adds an official SLO definition for the audit log delivery pipeline in slo-toolkit. The SLO covers (1) delivery latency and (2) delivery error rate (drops/failures), and includes error-budget burn alerts tuned for enterprise-tier expectations.

Context / why
As we standardize audit logging for Hosted/Dedicated/Private, audit delivery becomes a compliance-relevant pipeline. We need a single SLO that SRE + Security Eng can reference, and that we can consistently deploy across regions/environments. This SLO is meant to pair with the new audit pipeline dashboards/alerts work (observability-pack) and reduce alert noise by tightening conditions for enterprise while keeping standard tiers actionable.

What’s included
- New SLO: audit_log_delivery
  - Latency SLI: fraction of audit events delivered to the ingestion backend within threshold (derived from audit delivery latency histogram)
  - Error-rate SLI: fraction of audit events not dropped/failed (derived from drop counters + failed delivery counters)
- Tiering
  - enterprise: stricter latency threshold and tighter error budget
  - standard: slightly looser latency threshold to prevent paging during known backlog catch-ups
- Burn alerts
  - Multi-window burn alerts (fast/slow) per tier
  - Enterprise alerts page earlier (lower tolerated burn) but require sustained burn to avoid flapping
- Runbook linkage
  - Each alert annotation includes the canonical runbook link + dashboard link (populated by our standard alert templates)

SLO definition (high-level)
Latency objective
- enterprise: 99.0% of events delivered within 60s over 28d
- standard: 99.0% of events delivered within 120s over 28d

Error objective
- enterprise: 99.95% successful delivery (<= 0.05% drop/fail) over 28d
- standard: 99.9% successful delivery (<= 0.1% drop/fail) over 28d

SLI sources / metrics
- audit_delivery_latency_seconds_bucket (histogram) with labels: env, region, deployment_mode, tenant_tier
- audit_events_emitted_total and audit_events_delivered_total
- audit_events_dropped_total (includes overflow/backpressure drops) and audit_delivery_failures_total (downstream non-2xx, auth errors)

Notes
- We intentionally scope SLO to platform delivery into our audit ingestion backend, not downstream customer SIEM targets (HEC/Sentinel). Exporters will get separate SLOs once we have consistent per-destination success metrics.
- The SLO is label-aware for deployment_mode=hosted|dedicated|private but the default dashboards/alerts roll up per env+region and slice by tenant_tier.

Files changed
- slo/audit/audit_log_delivery.slo.yaml: new SLO spec
- slo/audit/README.md: rationale + how to interpret latency vs error SLIs
- alerts/audit/audit_log_delivery.alerts.yaml: new burn-rate alerts per tier
- templates/alert-annotations.libsonnet: add runbook/dashboard placeholders for audit SLOs
- docs/slo-catalog.md: register audit_log_delivery in catalog
- tests/golden/audit_log_delivery.*: golden output for rule generation

Rollout plan
- After merge, SRE will deploy to staging first (hosted + one private fixture) and validate burn alerts are quiet under baseline load.
- Production rollout in two waves: non-enterprise first, then enterprise tier after 48h of stable metrics.

Related
- Linear: ENG-4835 (drop-rate/latency alerts), ENG-4829 (retry/buffering)
- Dashboards/alerts pack: observability-pack PR-433

Checklist
- [x] SLO spec validated by slo-toolkit linter
- [x] Golden tests updated
- [x] Alert labels include service ownership + oncall routing
- [x] Annotations include runbook + dashboard links
- [x] Reviewed thresholds with Security Eng and SRE

Open questions (tracked in follow-up)
- Should enterprise latency be 30s once buffering is fully rolled out in Private? (follow-up in ENG-4829)
- Need consistent definition for ‘delivered’ vs ‘accepted’ once ingestion adds async write-ahead log metrics (future work)

title: Metering data quality SLO template (freshness + correctness) with example alerts
preview:
Context
We’ve had recurring issues where metering tables are “up” but not trustworthy: late-arriving events, duplicates due to retry/backfill paths, and dimensional nulls (e.g., region) causing downstream drift vs billing ledger. Finance/RevRec needs a consistent, auditable definition for: (1) how fresh billing datasets must be to be used for invoice preview / month-end close and (2) what correctness signals we monitor to catch billing-impacting issues early.

This PR adds a reusable SLO template focused on metering data quality, intended to be imported by services/pipelines that publish billing-critical tables (usage events, reconciliation exports, and ledger join views). The template standardizes:
- Freshness SLIs/SLOs (time since last complete partition; late event window)
- Correctness signals (drift vs ledger, duplicate event rate, null/missing dimension rate)
- Example alert rules with paging vs ticket thresholds
- Required runbook annotations and ownership metadata

What’s included
1) New template: metering_data_quality.yaml
- Freshness SLI: max(data_lag_minutes) over required partitions
- Drift SLI: abs(usage_events_cost - billing_ledger_cost) / billing_ledger_cost (aggregated by day + account + model + region)
- Duplicate-rate SLI: duplicate_event_count / total_event_count
- Null-dimension SLI: null_region_count / total_event_count (pattern supports other dimensions)

2) Alert policy defaults
- Freshness paging: > 90 minutes lag for “invoice-preview” tables (business hours), > 180 minutes otherwise
- Drift paging: > 0.75% for >= 2 consecutive evaluation periods OR > 2.0% once (guarded by min volume)
- Duplicate-rate paging: > 0.30% for >= 30m
- Null-rate paging: > 0.10% for >= 30m

3) Example service config + docs
- examples/redwood-metering/usage-events-quality-slo.yaml shows a concrete instantiation with common dimensions, min-volume guards, and runbook links
- docs/metering-data-quality-slo.md documents how to wire the template into Grafana/Prometheus, and how to tune thresholds for pipeline-specific characteristics

Non-goals
- This does not attempt to define the canonical meter spec (token counting, retries semantics, etc.). That remains in Confluence + Redwood app code.
- This does not ship the actual drift queries for every table; it provides a standard interface and example patterns.

Operational notes
- The defaults aim to reduce alert noise via min-volume thresholds and “consecutive periods” gating.
- Runbook link convention: runbook_url must point to the owning team’s doc with triage + mitigation + escalation (Finance sign-off where applicable).

Testing
- Unit tests validate template rendering and schema validation.
- Example config is validated in CI as a fixture.

Rollout plan
- Land template in slo-toolkit.
- Redwood monorepo will adopt in the metering sanity check job + Grafana dashboards (tracked in ENG-4421/PM-1872).
- After adoption, tune thresholds per region/model volume once we have 2 weeks of baseline.

Security/Access
- No customer data included; examples use placeholder metrics and sample names.
- Intended consumers should ensure the underlying metrics/queries respect billing-data access controls.

References
- Linear: PM-1842 (define meter sanity KPIs), PM-1872 (metering change control), ENG-4421 (alert on meter drift vs ledger)
- Related PRs in redwood: pr-27450 (sanity check job), pr-27510 (recon export), pr-27574 (runbook links)

title: ephemeral-diagnostic-funnel-and-priority-playbook-serializer
Triage Wizard: ephemeral telemetry aggregator + guided playbook preview
preview: This PR introduces a 'Triage Wizard' micro-workflow for on-call responders that assembles a compact, ephemeral telemetry snapshot and renders a guided playbook preview pre-populated with suggested remedial steps. Motivation: reduce cognitive overhead during incident triage by surfacing only the most relevant metrics, traces, and recent errors for the alert context, and to let responders preview playbook steps with example commands before executing. The core pieces: 1) telemetry-aggregator service: background job that collects top-5 signals (latency p50/p95, error trends, recent traces, host counts, queue depth) and outputs a compressed JSON blob; 2) triage-preview UI: client component that renders the snapshot alongside a stepwise playbook with inline 'try this' commands; 3) CLI hook: ability to fetch ephemeral snapshot and run a local dry-run of suggested remediation; 4) safety and privacy filters: removes PII, strips long stack traces, and enforces sampling rates. Checklist: - [x] backend aggregator and schema - [x] UI preview component with mock data - [x] CLI fetcher and dry-run runner - [x] unit and e2e tests - [x] docs for oncall usage - [x] feature flag gating in prod. This description covers design notes, compatibility considerations, and rollout plan: start gated under feature flag 'triage_wizard_v1', enable for SRE alpha team, collect UX metrics and error rates for 2 weeks, then broader rollout. Related linear items and usage playbook included in linked tickets.

title: telemetry-guided pretriage sanity-checker and lightweight oncall hook
preview: Motivation: on-call teams spend time investigating noisy alerts that are detectable as instrumentation anomalies (missing series, stale exporters, or thin traffic windows) before starting a full incident triage. This PR adds a lightweight 'pretriage sanity checker' that runs a small set of telemetry probes when an alert is received and surfaces a short human-readable verdict and artifacts to the responder. Design summary: implement a probe runner (timeout-first, non-blocking) that evaluates a configurable probe set against Prometheus and tracing backends, computes a compact fingerprint, and emits a JSON snapshot + human summary. Ship a CLI hook and a webhook payload for incident-bot/slo-toolkit to call as part of pretriage. Safety and opt-in: default checks are safe read-only Prometheus queries and a trace existence probe; probes and timeouts are configurable per-team via runtime config. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout to 10% of responses for two weeks, converge on rule tweaks, then enable org-wide. Checklist: 1) implement probe runner and schema; 2) add incident-bot hook + webhook contract; 3) surface results in oncall brief (summary line + artifact link); 4) add docs and runbook snippets; 5) add metrics and dashboard for false-positive rate. Testing: unit tests for probe parsing and mock Prom queries; integration test that exercises the webhook path using a local Prometheus test server; end-to-end smoke in canary environment. Related work: builds on prior compact-snapshot work and the 'telemetry-sanity-prober' spike (ENG-1982) and coordinates with incident-bot runbook suggestions. Backwards compatibility: webhook payloads are additive; incident-bot falls back if pretriage fails or times out. Implementation notes: the probe runner uses a 1.5s per-probe soft timeout and an overall 5s hard timeout; queries are sampled at scrubbed time windows for PII safety. Observability: new metrics prefixed slo.pretriage.* (attempts, timeouts, probe_failures, judgments) and a dashboard template in docs/dashboards/pretriage.md. Migration steps: operators can toggle feature via SLO_TOOLKIT_PRETRIAGE_ENABLED and tune PROBE_SET_PATH. Security: no credentials stored; Prometheus read-only credentials documented. This PR description is a compact summary of the commits, files changed, review thread, and CI outcome.

title: Add rollout-scoped burn-rate endpoint (route/model_version/region) with query caching
preview:
Summary: This adds first-class support in slo-toolkit for querying burn-rate scoped to a rollout slice (route + model_version + region + optional customer_tier). The rollout controller needs to evaluate fast/slow burn windows against the exact scope of the rollout step; previously we only supported coarse service-level burn-rate and a handful of static labels, which caused both false positives (baseline traffic in other regions) and false negatives (rollout-only regression diluted by global volume). This PR introduces (1) a new endpoint that accepts rollout scope and returns multi-window burn rate + sample sizes, (2) a query-planning layer to avoid N+1 PromQL fanout, and (3) an in-process cache + singleflight to reduce backend load and smooth bursty controller polling.

API changes: - New endpoint: GET /v1/burn-rate/rollout?service=<svc>&route=<route>&slo=<slo_name>&model_version=<ver>&region=<region>&customer_tier=<tier>&windows=5m,30m (customer_tier optional; windows optional, default 5m,30m). - Existing endpoint /v1/burn-rate remains unchanged. - Response includes: burn_rate_by_window, error_budget_remaining, request_count_by_window, evaluation_timestamp, data_freshness_seconds, and query_fingerprint. Note: response schema is still JSON but this PR keeps it backward compatible by only adding fields to the rollout endpoint.

Implementation details: - Scope normalization: route/model_version/region/customer_tier are normalized and validated against an allowlist to avoid unbounded label injection. We reject scopes that contain regex meta characters, wildcard operators, or exceed length limits (route 128, model_version 64, region 32, tier 32). - Query planning: we build a single PromQL expression per window that computes both burn rate and request volume for the same scope, rather than issuing separate queries. This reduces total remote_read calls from 4->2 per evaluation (two windows). - Cache: adds an LRU+TTL cache keyed by (service, route, slo, model_version, region, tier, windows, time_bucket). TTL defaults to 15s; time_bucket floors to 10s to improve hit rate during controller polling. Uses singleflight to coalesce concurrent identical queries (common during step transitions). Cache entries store both result and remote error to prevent thundering herds during backend partial outages (short negative caching, 5s). - Staleness handling: surfaces data_freshness_seconds computed from the newest sample timestamp returned by the query; if freshness > 120s we return 200 but mark stale=true in headers (X-SLO-Data-Stale: true) and include freshness in the payload so the controller can treat it as “monitor stale” and suppress auto-actions per policy.

Performance impact: On staging load tests simulating 1k rollout evaluations/min across 50 scopes, remote query QPS dropped ~62% due to caching + singleflight. p95 endpoint latency improved from 420ms -> 180ms with the same backend.

Security/abuse considerations: Added strict input validation, label allowlist, max windows count=4, and max time range=1h. Also added basic rate-limit hooks (existing middleware) specifically for the rollout endpoint to avoid accidental runaway loops.

Testing: - Unit tests for scope validation, key normalization, and cache behavior (positive + negative caching). - Integration test with the fake prom backend ensures query composition stays stable and returns expected sample counts. - Added benchmark for cache hit/miss path and singleflight concurrency.

Rollout plan: - This is gated behind feature flag in the rollout controller (tracked in redwood PR-28458 / PR-28463). - Observability-pack dashboards/alerts will use the same query fingerprint to correlate evaluation events with backend queries.

Refs: Linear ENG-4826; depends on rollout controller wiring in redwood; aligns with ADR-0127 (dual-window burn-rate + min-sample gating) by returning both fast/slow windows and request volume in one response.

We reject scopes that contain regex meta characters, wildcard operators, or exceed length limits (route 128, model_version 64, region 32, tier 32). - Query planning: we build a single PromQL expression per window that computes both burn rate and request volume for the same scope, rather than issuing separate queries. This reduces total remote_read calls from 4->2 per evaluation (two windows). - Cache: adds an LRU+TTL cache keyed by (service, route, slo, model_version, region, tier, windows, time_bucket). TTL defaults to 15s; time_bucket floors to 10s to improve hit rate during controller polling. Uses singleflight to coalesce concurrent identical queries (common during step transitions). Cache entries store both result and remote error to prevent thundering herds during backend partial outages (short negative caching, 5s). - Staleness handling: surfaces data_freshness_seconds computed from the newest sample timestamp returned by the query; if freshness > 120s we return 200 but mark stale=true in headers (X-SLO-Data-Stale: true) and include freshness in the payload so the controller can treat it as “monitor stale” and suppress auto-actions per policy.

Performance impact: On staging load tests simulating 1k rollout evaluations/min across 50 scopes, remote query QPS dropped ~62% due to caching + singleflight. p95 endpoint latency improved from 420ms -> 180ms with the same backend.

Security/abuse considerations: Added strict input validation, label allowlist, max windows count=4, and max time range=1h. Also added basic rate-limit hooks (existing middleware) specifically for the rollout endpoint to avoid accidental runaway loops.

Testing: - Unit tests for scope validation, key normalization, and cache behavior (positive + negative caching). - Integration test with the fake prom backend ensures query composition stays stable and returns expected sample counts. - Added benchmark for cache hit/miss path and singleflight concurrency.

Rollout plan: - This is gated behind feature flag in the rollout controller (tracked in redwood PR-28458 / PR-28463). - Observability-pack dashboards/alerts will use the same query fingerprint to correlate evaluation events with backend queries.

Refs: Linear ENG-4826; depends on rollout controller wiring in redwood; aligns with ADR-0127 (dual-window burn-rate + min-sample gating) by returning both fast/slow windows and request volume in one response.

title: Add incident remediation SLA policy evaluator (due times + breach classification)
preview: Context: As part of the Reliability Program (Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping), we need a single, audited implementation for remediation SLA timers. Today, multiple systems (incident-bot, dashboards, ad-hoc scripts) are calculating “SLA due” inconsistently, and edge cases (pause/override, force majeure, customer-caused) are handled differently. This PR introduces a small, dependency-light library in slo-toolkit to: (1) compute due_at for remediation SLAs from incident metadata, (2) evaluate whether an incident is currently in breach / at risk, and (3) provide a stable, versioned policy surface we can share across services. Scope / Approach: - Adds @redwood/sla-policy (library) with a compact policy model that takes severity (Sev0–Sev3), tier (Hosted | Dedicated | Private), and taxonomy (incident_type + optional subtype) and returns: time_to_mitigate_minutes, time_to_fix_minutes, due_mitigate_at, due_fix_at, and breach status given timestamps. - Includes a CLI tool `sla-policy` for debugging and validation (used by SRE during rollout + backfills). - Policy is defined as a checked-in JSON allowlist (low-cardinality, v1 taxonomy only) to avoid runtime lookups and to ensure “unknown/unclassified” behavior is explicit. - Supports pause windows and overrides via an optional `adjustments` array (e.g., pause from incident_start->provider_outage_resolved). These are treated as auditable inputs; the evaluator does not infer pauses. - Adds explicit handling for: unknown incident_type, missing tier, and “customer-caused” incidents (requires an explicit flag; otherwise default policy applies). Key API: - `computeSla(incident, policy, now)` returns `SlaResult`. - `evaluateBreach(slaResult, now)` returns `ok | at_risk | breached` with remaining_minutes. - `validatePolicy(policy)` is run at startup/CLI and in CI to prevent unbounded growth and to ensure all keys match the v1 taxonomy allowlist. Example: `const r = computeSla({ incident_id: "INC-24891", started_at: "2026-02-02T19:22:00Z", severity: "SEV1", tier: "DEDICATED", incident_type: "capacity", subtype: "regional_gpu_shortage" }, defaultPolicy, new Date());` returns due timestamps aligned with the remediation SLA matrix, and then `evaluateBreach` provides `at_risk` vs `breached` given the current time. Notes / Alignment: - Policy values and key names align with Confluence “Owner mapping + remediation SLA matrix” and the incident-bot required taxonomy fields rollout. - This evaluator is used by downstream work (redwood PR-28635 breach alerting + incident-bot gating) to ensure consistent SLA timers across systems. Testing: - Unit tests cover due time computation, pause/override windows, unknown keys defaulting behavior, and a set of golden test vectors derived from 8 real incidents (anonymized) from the last 90 days backfill. - Adds a policy validation test that fails CI if new policy keys are added without updating the allowlist + docs. Rollout plan: - Land library first; consumers will vendor it via semver tag from slo-toolkit. - SRE will run CLI against a sample of recent incidents to verify due_at matches the matrix before enabling breach paging. - Follow-up will wire incident-bot to show due_at and “time remaining” in the incident channel header.

title: CLI: generate tiered SLO configs + validate metric coverage by tier/endpoint/region
preview: This PR adds CLI tooling to (1) generate tier-aware SLO config files from a canonical targets table and (2) validate metric coverage required to compute SLIs per tier/endpoint/region. This is intended to unblock the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program by making SLO config generation repeatable and preventing silent SLO data gaps (e.g., missing tier tags at ingest, missing streaming disconnect counters, or latency histograms not exported in a region). The generator produces configs compatible with the redwood SLO loader/schema introduced in redwood/pr-28438 (same field names and tier labeling). The validator checks that required metrics exist and are queryable for each config row, with guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions (no per-customer/per-model checks by default; validation is done at metric+label-key level). The CLI is intended for CI usage (fail fast) and for on-call debugging (show what’s missing + suggested instrumentation owners).

title: Add error budget burn-rate alerts for enterprise protected-route SLOs
preview:
## Context
This PR adds burn-rate alerting presets to `slo-toolkit` for Redwood Hosted API enterprise protected routes as part of the Hot-route capacity protection initiative. The intent is to page on *fast* error-budget consumption for enterprise SLOs (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, embeddings, rerank) without generating paging noise during expected best-effort shedding.

Motivation:
- Prior incident and game-day findings showed we need early, reliable paging on *enterprise* SLO risk rather than generic fleet-wide latency/5xx signals.
- With tier-aware throttling/admission control rolling out, we expect 429s on best-effort; burn alerts should focus on enterprise SLI outcomes.

Links:
- Linear: ENG-4926 (Dashboards/alerts for protected routes), PM-882 (Enterprise SLO policy)
- Related PRs (for context): redwood/pr-27441 (429 reason codes), observability-pack/pr-1832 (dashboards)

## What this changes
### 1) New alert presets: enterprise protected routes
Adds new SLO alert rule templates for enterprise route SLOs using multi-window, multi-burn-rate (MWMB) patterns.

Implemented presets:
- `enterprise_protected_route_availability_burn` (availability SLOs)
- `enterprise_protected_route_latency_burn` (latency SLOs)

Windows/burn multipliers (defaults; can be overridden per SLO):
- Page (critical): 5m @ 14x AND 1h @ 14x (fast burn), plus 30m @ 6x AND 6h @ 6x (sustained burn)
- Ticket (warning): 2h @ 3x AND 1d @ 3x

Rationale for numbers:
- Mirrors common SRE MWMB guidance while being tuned for Redwood’s “protected routes” where a small amount of budget burn is meaningful.
- Avoids paging on short spikes that do not materially threaten monthly budget.

### 2) Labeling + routing guidance to keep cardinality bounded
Adds explicit guidance and guardrails in rule generation:
- Requires `route_group` rather than raw `path` labels for alert grouping.
- Allows `model_family` only where it’s a small bounded set; discourages `tenant_id`/`api_key` labels.

### 3) Per-route overrides without copy/paste
Introduces a small override mechanism:
- Defaults live in one place (enterprise protected route presets)
- Individual SLO definitions may override thresholds/windows via `alerting.overrides.*` fields in the SLO spec

### 4) Documentation updates
Adds a short doc page describing:
- When to use enterprise protected route burn alerts
- Recommended paging policy and ownership (SRE vs Platform on-call)
- Example config for chat/embeddings/rerank

## Example
Added an example SLO snippet:
- `route_group: chat_completions`
- `tier: enterprise`
- Applies the new burn presets and routes to `pagerduty_service: hosted-api-enterprise`

## Testing
- Unit tests for rule rendering (ensures correct PromQL shape, annotation fields, and routing labels)
- Golden tests updated for newly generated alert rules
- Lint (yaml/json schema) + CI pass

## Rollout / operational notes
- This PR does not enable alerts by itself; it updates toolkit templates consumed by the Observability Pack.
- Rollout should be coordinated with observability-pack/pr-1832 and the hosted API SLO definitions doc so we don’t page on incomplete SLI wiring.

## Checklist
- [x] Added presets + unit tests
- [x] Added docs and example SLO definition
- [x] Ensured label guidance avoids high-cardinality tags
- [x] Confirmed alert naming aligns with observability standards

---
## Changes (high-level)
- Add new enterprise protected-route burn-rate presets (availability + latency)
- Add override mechanism for windows/thresholds
- Add docs + example
- Update golden test fixtures

## Commits
1) slo-toolkit: add enterprise protected route burn presets
2) slo-toolkit: add override plumbing and schema validation
3) slo-toolkit: docs + examples
4) tests: update golden fixtures and add render tests

## Review notes / discussion summary
- Sergio: Requested that the 5m/1h critical windows be gated by a sustained pair to reduce paging on short-lived deploy blips. Implemented MWMB pairs and added runbook link placeholder in annotations.
- Allison: Flagged risk of `path` label cardinality. Updated templates to require `route_group` and added validation that rejects raw `path` for this preset.
- Logan: Asked to ensure we don’t treat 429s as failures for enterprise SLOs unless explicitly configured. Added doc note: enterprise SLI should count successful responses; 429s should be separated via reason codes and not mixed into error budget unless the SLO definition chooses to.
- Rafael: Confirmed thresholds match game-day outcomes; requested annotation fields include route_group/tier to make incident-bot messages readable. Added annotations and standardized labels.

## CI
Checks:
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- golden-tests: pass
- lint/schema: pass

Merge:
- Squash merged into `main` after approvals and green CI.

title: oncall-memory-journal-and-telemetry-coalescer
preview:
Motivation: On-call responders frequently need quick access to recent human decisions, triage notes, and distilled telemetry trends. This PR introduces a lightweight oncall-memory journal plus a telemetry coalescer to reduce noisy signal volume and surface concise, actionable summaries to the incident-bot and slo-toolkit dashboards.

What this change does (single-string description): Adds an append-only oncall memory service (daemon + small embedded store) that journals short annotations from responders and automated synthesis hooks; provides a compact HTTP API (/v1/memory/append, /v1/memory/query) and a CLI (slo-memory export --range) for exporting session summaries. Implements a telemetry coalescer that consumes existing telemetry streams (metrics/traces/log-keys) and produces pre-aggregated "event shards" using configurable fingerprinting rules (by service, alert-key, tag-set) and time-windowed coalescing (default 20s). The coalescer exposes a lightweight local cache used by the console and incident-bot to render condensed trendlines and sampled exemplars. Privacy and safety: the memory journal applies a redaction pipeline (PII regexes + allowlist for tokens) and emits audit events to the existing audit-log exporter.

Implementation notes: The memory daemon is implemented in Go, embedded BoltDB-backed append journal with a capped retention policy (configurable: default 72h). The daemon accepts JSON payloads limited to 1KB per entry and indexes by incident_id, responder_id, and source. The coalescer is implemented as a small Rust service (for low-latency memcoalescing) that subscribes to the metrics bus, applies coalescing heuristics defined in YAML, and writes rollups to Redis for fast reads. We've added a new adapter in incident-bot that will call the memory API when a responder posts a quick note or marks a step as 'confirmed'.

Backwards compatibility: services not using the new adapter see no change. Memory endpoints are behind a feature flag (slo_memory.enabled). Coalescer can run in a passive mode that doesn't write rollups for clusters that prefer external aggregation.

Testing and safety: unit tests for the redaction pipeline and coalescing heuristics included, integration tests for the CLI export and incident-bot adapter added. Benchmarks show <2ms median append latency and coalescer ingestion sustaining 10k events/s on the configured test harness.

Docs and migration: Added docs/operations/oncall-memory.md and a short quickstart in docs/cli/README.md. Migration notes outline a two-week opt-in pilot and an emergency purge command for compliance.

Why this is different: unlike previous snapshot exporters, this focuses on low-latency, small-footprint oncall memory with explicit privacy controls and a telemetry coalescer tuned for on-call consumption (short windows, exemplar preservation).

title: Add rollback gate rule library (error-rate + p95 latency) with defaults + overrides
preview:
Context: As part of Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs, we need a consistent way to evaluate rollout gates across services/environments. Prior implementations hard-coded thresholds and metric queries per rollout controller, which made it difficult to tune defaults, apply consistent tagging requirements (tenant/model/region/config_version), and explain gate decisions in the Console/runbooks.

This PR adds a small, reusable “gate rule library” to slo-toolkit with two initial rules used by the canary rollout controller:
- Error rate (5xx) gate
- p95 latency gate

Key points:
- Sane defaults aimed at Hosted/Dedicated canaries, with explicit override hooks for Dedicated/private installs.
- A single evaluation result object that includes a user-facing explanation string, the metric query/selector used, the time window, and the exact threshold evaluated.
- Strict tag requirements (tenant, model, region, config_version) with a “fail closed” option for production rollouts (default), and “warn only” for staging.
- Guardrails to reduce false positives: minimum request volume check + burn-in period support.

The rollout controller (redwood repo) will consume these rules in a follow-up PR; this PR is scoped to slo-toolkit only.

Testing:
- Added unit tests covering: threshold parsing, minimum sample gating, tag validation, override precedence, and explanation formatting.
- Added golden tests for rendered queries to make refactors safer.

Rollout:
- No behavior change until the rollout controller switches to these rules.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4827 (SLO gates: latency and 5xx), ENG-4824 (canary rollout controller)
- Related: perf-canary gate signals and dashboard tagging work (config_version annotations).

title: Add per-model performance SLO templates (p95 latency + tokens/sec) for perf-canary + rollout gates
preview: This PR introduces first-class SLO Toolkit templates for per-model performance signals that we can reuse across regions/tier pools and plug into perf-canary alerting + rollout gates. We currently have ad-hoc Grafana + PromQL alerts in observability-pack that are hard to keep consistent, and they don't encode our recommended dampening/min-samples patterns. These templates are intended to standardize how we express and reason about performance regressions (latency and throughput) without turning performance into a classical "error budget" SLO. Scope: (1) Add new template families: ModelLatencyRegression and ModelThroughputRegression. (2) Provide guidance in-template for threshold selection (per model + SKU + region), minimum sample size, and dampening windows. (3) Add a small helper to keep label cardinality safe (standard label allowlist + enforced aggregation). (4) Add example configs for perf-canary Phase 1 (us-east) so SRE + Runtime can validate before enabling paging. Definitions: Latency regression signal = observed p95 end-to-end latency compared to a stored baseline p95 for the same model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hw_sku (+ optional region) for the same prompt-shape profile; regression computed as percent change and gated by minimum sample size over a rolling evaluation window. Throughput regression signal = observed tokens/sec (decode-dominant) compared to baseline tokens/sec for same dimensions; computed similarly. Templates intentionally avoid customer prompt data by only consuming perf-canary synthetic traffic metrics and baseline artifacts. Guidance included (docs) covers: recommended initial thresholds (warn at +10% p95 latency or -10% toks/sec; page at +20% or -20% sustained), how to tune per model based on variance, when to split thresholds by region/SKU, and how to avoid alert storms due to baseline drift or label explosions. Follow-ups: observability-pack PRs will consume these templates and remove duplicated alert rules. This does not change any existing production alerts by itself; it only adds templates and examples.
text: This PR introduces first-class SLO Toolkit templates for per-model performance signals that we can reuse across regions/tier pools and plug into perf-canary alerting + rollout gates. We currently have ad-hoc Grafana + PromQL alerts in observability-pack that are hard to keep consistent, and they don't encode our recommended dampening/min-samples patterns. These templates are intended to standardize how we express and reason about performance regressions (latency and throughput) without turning performance into a classical "error budget" SLO. Scope: (1) Add new template families: ModelLatencyRegression and ModelThroughputRegression. (2) Provide guidance in-template for threshold selection (per model + SKU + region), minimum sample size, and dampening windows. (3) Add a small helper to keep label cardinality safe (standard label allowlist + enforced aggregation). (4) Add example configs for perf-canary Phase 1 (us-east) so SRE + Runtime can validate before enabling paging. Definitions: Latency regression signal = observed p95 end-to-end latency compared to a stored baseline p95 for the same model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hw_sku (+ optional region) for the same prompt-shape profile; regression computed as percent change and gated by minimum sample size over a rolling evaluation window. Throughput regression signal = observed tokens/sec (decode-dominant) compared to baseline tokens/sec for same dimensions; computed similarly. Templates intentionally avoid customer prompt data by only consuming perf-canary synthetic traffic metrics and baseline artifacts. Guidance included (docs) covers: recommended initial thresholds (warn at +10% p95 latency or -10% toks/sec; page at +20% or -20% sustained), how to tune per model based on variance, when to split thresholds by region/SKU, and how to avoid alert storms due to baseline drift or label explosions. Follow-ups: observability-pack PRs will consume these templates and remove duplicated alert rules. This does not change any existing production alerts by itself; it only adds templates and examples.

title: Add weekly status accuracy audit job (incident tags vs Statuspage updates)
preview: Motivation: We’ve had recurring cases where the public status page didn’t reflect the impacted capability/region (e.g., streaming-only or embeddings-only degradations) even though the incident was tagged correctly internally, and vice-versa. This PR adds a lightweight weekly audit job to detect mismatches between (a) incident metadata (component_id/region/capability + severity + impact window) and (b) the Statuspage incident updates/components that were actually published. The goal is not to page or create more on-call work; it’s to produce a small, actionable report for follow-up during business hours and feed the status page transparency success metric (accuracy audits). What it does: 1) Runs weekly (Mon 09:00 PT) as a Kubernetes CronJob in the internal tooling cluster. 2) Pulls incidents from the incident timeline export endpoint (incident-bot export) for the prior 7 days, filtering to Sev0/Sev1 and any Sev2 that included a customer-facing status update flag. 3) Pulls corresponding Statuspage incidents/updates for the same time window using the internal Statuspage API client credentials (read-only). 4) Compares: (a) component_id list (including region/capability split), (b) first public update timestamp vs incident start (TTFU heuristic), and (c) whether the incident was marked resolved on Statuspage within 60 minutes of internal resolution. 5) Emits a report to #status-accuracy-audits (and #eng-sre on >N mismatches) with links to the incident, the Statuspage incident, and a short “likely fix” hint (e.g., missing region tag, component map mismatch, anti-flap prevented update, wrong capability selected). 6) Exposes Prometheus metrics for trend tracking (mismatch_count, ttfu_minutes_p50/p90, missing_component_tag_count) and adds a Grafana panel link in the output. Scope/Non-goals: This does not auto-edit Statuspage, does not create Linear tickets automatically (by design), and does not attempt to infer “true customer impact” from raw SLO data; it only checks consistency between internal incident tags and what we published. Safety/Noise controls: (a) Uses a allowlist of components that are currently GA on the public status page; anything behind rollout flags is reported as “skipped” to avoid false positives during rollout. (b) Has a minimum-duration threshold (default 5 min) before considering a mismatch relevant. (c) Dedupes repeated incidents due to Statuspage edits. (d) Stores the last audit cursor (time window) in a small ConfigMap to avoid double-reporting on reruns. Testing: Unit tests cover component normalization, time-window matching, and dedupe logic. Integration tests use recorded fixtures for both incident-bot export and Statuspage API responses. Rollout plan: Deploy to staging for 1 week with reports going to #status-accuracy-audits-staging, then enable prod schedule. Related: This supports the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and complements component mapping/schema work (ENG-4830) and incident-bot statuspage integration (ENG-4822).
